subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — EOG Practice Test 2
instructions: Read each question carefully. Choose the best answer.

passages.title: The Lost Mitten
passages.text:
Maya pulled on her boots and raced outside to play in the first snow of winter. The whole yard sparkled white, and her breath made little clouds in the cold air. She packed a snowball and tossed it high, laughing as it broke apart over her head.

When Maya finally went back inside, her mother frowned. "Where is your other mitten?" she asked. Maya looked down at her hands. One mitten was bright red and warm. The other was gone. Her heart sank. Those mittens were a gift from her grandmother, who had knitted them by hand.

Maya bundled up again and went back out to search. She walked slowly across the yard, looking under every bush and beside every snow pile. The wind had picked up, and fresh snow was beginning to fall. Soon her tracks from before were covered, and the red mitten was nowhere to be seen.

Just as Maya was about to give up, she heard a soft chirp. A small brown sparrow hopped near the old oak tree. The bird kept flying up to a low branch and back down again, as if it wanted Maya to follow. Curious, Maya walked toward the tree.

There, tucked into the fork of a branch, was a cozy nest. And woven right into the middle of it was a flash of bright red yarn. The sparrow had found her lost mitten first and pulled out bits of the soft wool to line its home for the winter.

Maya smiled. She could not take the yarn back now, not when the little bird needed it to stay warm. "You keep it," she whispered. That night she told her grandmother the whole story. Her grandmother laughed warmly and promised to knit a brand-new pair, this time with enough extra yarn for the sparrows too.

passages.title: The Crow and the Pitcher
passages.text:
Long ago, on a hot and dusty day, a thirsty crow flew over the dry land searching for water. The sun beat down, and the crow's throat felt as dry as sand. For hours she found nothing but cracked earth and empty streams.

At last, near the edge of a quiet garden, the crow spotted a tall clay pitcher. She flew down at once and peered inside. There was water at the bottom, but the pitcher was tall and narrow. No matter how far the crow stretched her neck, she could not reach the cool water below.

The crow did not give up. She thought hard about the problem. Then she noticed a pile of small pebbles lying on the ground nearby. An idea came to her, and her eyes grew bright.

One by one, the crow picked up a pebble in her beak and dropped it into the pitcher. Plink. Plink. Plink. With each stone she added, the water rose just a little higher. The crow worked patiently, never stopping, even though her wings grew tired.

After many pebbles, the water at last rose high enough to reach. The crow dipped her beak and drank deeply, feeling the cool water soothe her dry throat. She had solved her problem not with strength, but with a clever mind.

The old fable teaches us a lesson that is still true today. When a problem seems too hard at first, a calm and thoughtful plan can succeed where rushing and force would fail. Little by little, steady effort can do great things.

passages.title: How Bees Make Honey
passages.text:
Honeybees are tiny insects that do an amazing job. They turn the sweet liquid inside flowers into the golden honey that many people enjoy. Making honey takes teamwork, hard work, and many trips between the flowers and the hive.

It all begins when a worker bee leaves the hive to find flowers. The bee uses a long, tube-like tongue to sip a sugary liquid called nectar from deep inside the blossom. The bee stores the nectar in a special pouch in its body called the honey stomach. A single bee may visit more than a hundred flowers before flying home.

Back at the hive, the busy bee passes the nectar to other worker bees. These bees chew the nectar for a while, which begins to change it. Then they spread the nectar into the waxy cells of the honeycomb. The honeycomb is made of many small six-sided spaces where the bees store their food.

The fresh nectar is still very watery, so the bees must dry it out. They beat their wings quickly to fan the cells, almost like little fans. As air moves over the nectar, the extra water slowly disappears. What is left behind becomes thick, sticky honey.

Finally, the bees seal each full cell with a thin cap of wax to keep the honey fresh. The bees save this honey to eat during the cold winter when no flowers bloom. A strong hive can store far more honey than the bees need, and beekeepers carefully collect the extra to share with us.

passages.title: Wilma Rudolph: The Fastest Woman
passages.text:
Wilma Rudolph was born in Tennessee in 1940. She was a very small and sickly baby, and few people thought she would grow up to be strong. As a young child, Wilma became very ill with a disease called polio. The illness left her left leg weak, and she had to wear a heavy metal brace just to walk.

Doctors told Wilma that she might never walk normally again. But Wilma was determined to prove them wrong. Her large family helped her every day. Her brothers and sisters took turns rubbing her weak leg, and her mother drove her many miles to see special doctors.

Little by little, Wilma grew stronger. By the time she was twelve years old, she no longer needed the brace at all. She joined her school's basketball and track teams and quickly showed that she could run faster than almost anyone. The girl who once could not walk had become a star athlete.

In 1960, Wilma traveled to Rome, Italy, to compete in the Olympic Games. There she ran in three races, and she won every single one. Wilma became the first American woman ever to win three gold medals in track at one Olympics. The whole world cheered for the runner from Tennessee.

After her racing days ended, Wilma became a teacher and a coach. She started a program to help young athletes follow their dreams. Wilma Rudolph showed everyone that with courage and hard work, a person can overcome even the greatest challenges.

questions.questionText: Why does Maya go back outside after coming inside?
options: To search for her lost mitten
questions.explanation: Maya bundles up and goes back out to search for the missing red mitten.

questions.questionText: Who made the mittens that Maya wears?
questions.explanation: The passage says the mittens were a gift from her grandmother, who knitted them by hand.

questions.questionText: Why does Maya have trouble finding her tracks from before?
options: The sparrow had brushed them away
Her mother had cleaned the yard
questions.explanation: The passage says fresh snow began to fall and soon her earlier tracks were covered.

questions.questionText: In paragraph 2, the words "Her heart sank" mean that Maya felt —
questions.explanation: "Her heart sank" shows Maya felt sad and worried about losing the special mitten.

questions.questionText: Why does the sparrow keep flying up to the branch and back down?
options: It wants Maya to follow it to the nest
It is trying to catch a snowflake
It is looking for food on the ground
questions.explanation: The bird flies up and down as if it wants Maya to follow, leading her to the nest.

questions.questionText: What had the sparrow done with the red mitten?
options: Dropped it in a snow pile
Used bits of its yarn to line a nest
questions.explanation: The sparrow pulled out bits of the soft wool yarn to line its nest for winter.

questions.questionText: What is the main message of the story?
options: Snow always melts in the end
Being kind can mean letting go of something you love
It is wrong to play in the snow
questions.explanation: Maya kindly lets the sparrow keep the yarn it needs, showing kindness sometimes means giving something up.

questions.questionText: How does Maya most likely feel at the end of the story?
questions.explanation: Maya smiles, lets the bird keep the yarn, and shares the story warmly, showing she feels happy and kind.

questions.questionText: Select all that apply. Which details show that the red mittens were special to Maya?
options: They were a gift from her grandmother
Her heart sank when one was lost
questions.explanation: The mittens were a handmade gift from grandmother, and Maya's heart sank when one was lost. They were not store-bought.

questions.questionText: What problem does the crow have at the beginning of the fable?
options: She is lost in a garden
She is very thirsty and cannot find water
questions.explanation: The crow is thirsty and flies over dry land searching for water.

questions.questionText: Why can't the crow reach the water in the pitcher at first?
options: The pitcher is tall and narrow
The pitcher has a lid on top
questions.explanation: The pitcher is tall and narrow, so the crow cannot stretch her neck far enough to reach the water.

questions.questionText: How does the crow finally get a drink?
options: She drops pebbles in to raise the water
She waits for rain to fill it
She breaks the pitcher with her beak
questions.explanation: The crow drops pebbles into the pitcher one by one, which makes the water rise high enough to reach.

questions.questionText: How many pebbles does the crow drop into the pitcher at one time? Write the number.
questions.explanation: The passage says the crow picked up the pebbles one by one, so she drops one pebble at a time.

questions.questionText: In paragraph 4, the word "patiently" tells the reader that the crow worked —
questions.explanation: Working patiently means staying calm and not giving up, which the crow did even when her wings grew tired.

questions.questionText: What does the word "Plink" most likely describe?
options: The sound of a pebble landing in the water
questions.explanation: "Plink" is the sound each pebble makes as it drops into the water in the pitcher.

questions.questionText: What lesson does this fable teach?
options: A clever, steady plan can solve a hard problem
Never drink water from a pitcher
Birds are stronger than other animals
questions.explanation: The fable says a calm, thoughtful plan and steady effort can succeed where force would fail.

questions.questionText: Why does the author end the fable with a paragraph about a lesson?
options: To describe the garden in more detail
To explain the meaning the story teaches
questions.explanation: The last paragraph states the lesson, which is the point of a fable.

questions.questionText: Which sentence best shows that the crow did not give up?
options: "The sun beat down, and the crow's throat felt as dry as sand."
"She flew down at once and peered inside."
"The crow worked patiently, never stopping, even though her wings grew tired."
"There was water at the bottom, but the pitcher was tall and narrow."
questions.explanation: Working without stopping even when tired shows the crow did not give up.

questions.questionText: What is this article mostly about?
questions.explanation: The whole article explains the steps bees take to turn nectar into honey.

questions.questionText: In paragraph 2, the word "nectar" means —
questions.explanation: The passage says nectar is the sugary liquid the bee sips from deep inside the blossom.

questions.questionText: Where does a worker bee store nectar while flying back to the hive?
questions.explanation: The bee stores the nectar in a special pouch called the honey stomach.

questions.questionText: How does the author mainly organize the information in this article?
options: By comparing two kinds of bees
By describing the steps in order from start to finish
By telling a story about one bee's day
questions.explanation: The article moves in order: finding nectar, passing it, drying it, and sealing the honey.

questions.questionText: Why do the bees beat their wings over the honeycomb cells?
options: To dry out the extra water in the nectar
To call other bees to the hive
questions.explanation: Beating their wings fans the cells so the extra water disappears, leaving thick honey.

questions.questionText: According to the article, how many sides does each space in the honeycomb have? Write the number.
questions.explanation: The article describes the honeycomb cells as small six-sided spaces.

questions.questionText: Why is it important that bees seal the honey with a cap of wax?
options: To make the honey a brighter color
To keep the honey fresh for the winter
questions.explanation: The bees seal the cells to keep the honey fresh so they can eat it in winter when no flowers bloom.

questions.questionText: Select all that apply. Which steps do bees take to make honey?
options: Fan the cells to dry the nectar
questions.explanation: Bees sip nectar, fan the cells to dry it, and seal them with wax. They do not paint the honeycomb.

questions.questionText: Based on the article, why can beekeepers collect honey without harming the bees?
options: Bees do not really need honey
A strong hive stores more honey than the bees need
Bees make new honey every hour
questions.explanation: The article says a strong hive can store far more honey than the bees need, so beekeepers take the extra.

questions.questionText: What illness did Wilma Rudolph have as a young child?
questions.explanation: The passage says Wilma became very ill with a disease called polio.

questions.questionText: What did Wilma have to wear in order to walk as a child?
questions.explanation: Polio left her left leg weak, and she had to wear a heavy metal brace to walk.

questions.questionText: Select all that apply. How did Wilma's family help her get stronger?
options: Her brothers and sisters rubbed her weak leg
Her mother drove her to special doctors
They built her a running track
They paid for new metal braces
questions.explanation: Her siblings took turns rubbing her weak leg, and her mother drove her many miles to special doctors.

questions.questionText: How many gold medals did Wilma win in track at the 1960 Olympics? Write the number.
questions.explanation: Wilma ran in three races and won every one, becoming the first American woman to win three gold medals in track at one Olympics.

questions.questionText: In paragraph 2, the word "determined" means that Wilma was —
questions.explanation: Being determined means she was firm about her goal to prove the doctors wrong and walk again.

questions.questionText: How is this passage mainly organized?
options: By telling the events of Wilma's life in time order
By comparing Wilma to other runners
By listing reasons polio is dangerous
questions.explanation: The passage follows Wilma's life in order, from her birth and illness to her Olympic wins and later work.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of this passage?
options: Polio is a common childhood illness
Wilma Rudolph overcame great challenges to become a champion
The Olympics are held in different cities
Running is the best sport to play
questions.explanation: The passage shows how Wilma overcame illness and hardship through courage and hard work to become an Olympic champion.

questions.questionText: Why did the author most likely include the last paragraph about Wilma becoming a coach?
options: To show that running is easy
To show she kept helping others after her racing days
To explain how to win a gold medal
To describe the city of Rome
questions.explanation: The last paragraph shows Wilma went on to teach, coach, and help young athletes follow their dreams.

questions.questionText: Which sentence best supports the idea that Wilma did not let doctors discourage her?
options: "Wilma Rudolph was born in Tennessee in 1940."
"But Wilma was determined to prove them wrong."
"The whole world cheered for the runner from Tennessee."
"She was a very small and sickly baby."
questions.explanation: Being determined to prove the doctors wrong shows she did not let their words discourage her.
